speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Paula Weitzman. Today's date is September the 6th, 1996. I will be conducting an interview in English in Montreal, Canada, with Mr. Philip Katz. Can you please tell me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Philip E. Katz, P-H-L-I-- P-H-I-L-I-P E K-A-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the E stands for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ephraim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-P-H-R-A-I-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Fulop, F-U umlaut L-O umlaut P.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fulop. And the family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually I shouldn't say that because there was a period of time in the United States when for a funny reason I was known as Mr. Philip because there was another man by the name of Franz Joseph Katz in the same office that I was at who preceded me. And so it was decided to avoid confusion that I would be called Mr. Ephraim Philip until the man, Franz Joseph Katz, was transferred. And I could recover my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the years from 1939 to 1945, you were known as--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was known as Fulop Katz. And familiarly with most people in my town, known as [? Fischu, ?] which was very kind of a distortion of my Yiddish name, which was [? Fischel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 1, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Kisvarda, K-I-S-V-A-R-D-A, in Hungary. It was, for Hungary, a medium-sized town of about 18,000 to 20,000, and served as an administrative and market center in the county of Sabolcs, S-A-B-A-- B-O, I'm sorry, B-O-L-C-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall your address in Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. It was 7 hyphen 9 Saint Laszlo. It's L-A-S-Z-L-O-A-- L-O, I'm sorry, Street, which was the main street in Kisvarda. And we were-- our home was located virtually dead center in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe Kisvarda for me and the Jewish life that existed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's easier said than done. Kisvarda, as I said had, about 18,000 to 20,000 population. And the Jewish population constituted almost one third of the population of, of the town. It was one of those relatively few towns in Hungary where the Jewish population, other than in Budapest-- where, of course, over a quarter of the population was Jewish-- where there was such a heavy concentration of Jewish population and where the impact of the Jewish community was as substantial as it was in Kisvarda. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Almost all of commerce [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] professions-- banking, what little manufacturing there was, and other than the schools, I would say all of cultural life, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] including the lending libraries-- which is the only kind of libraries that were in town; there were no public libraries-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were completely Jewishly dominated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jewish community itself was a very interesting and composite community. It was entirely Orthodox. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But, of course, the actual population, Jewish population, wasn't Orthodox by any means. They were both various shading of observance as well as various shadings of assimilation, various degrees of assimilation. But formally, the community was Orthodox. And, as a matter of fact, I would say that the community was, by and large, dominated by the fundamentalist or the extreme Orthodox, mostly Hasidic community. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In, in its public behavior, the community had to conform to the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] positions basically-- or observance basically [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] prescribed by the Ultra Orthodox. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know for sure whether or not numerically they also constituted the majority, although it could have been because as, as usual in-- with orthodox communities, their birth rate, of course, was much higher than that of the non-orthodox or non-observant or assimilated Jews. So it is quite possible that, that they constituted the majority. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But what is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most significant is that, that they dominated the, the coloration of, of the community. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The community in Kisvarda was very much like any other Orthodox Hungarian community in terms of its organizations and institutions. There was a council, which was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by and large, representative of all shades of opinion. They were elected. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The community was officially recognized and the government's power was available to collect what was known as [NON-ENGLISH] Tax, which is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the official tax that, that everyone had to pay to a religious community, whether Christian or, or Jewish. This was, by and large, I think, true of all Central European countries, certainly, after the Enlightenment. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The-- out of this-- these taxes, the community, of course, maintained a variety of institutions, starting from the kosher slaughtering of animals and fowl to the rabbis, the cantors, the ritual bath. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a very large Jewish public school, which I believe went up to either six or eight grades-- I think maybe eight grades. The school, of course, had to have a core curriculum conforming to the prescribed-- the government-prescribed curriculum. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was relatively little Jewish studies at, at this school. Jewish studies were to be acquired in the-- in the religious schools, the cheders and the Talmud Torah and the yeshiva, which also functioned from time to time, not always, dependent on who the rabbi was. There were, in the city, I would say, at least two dozen permanent, functioning synagogues. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were the major three synagogues or the central synagogue, which was a very large and quite beautiful edifice, built probably in the late '20s, maybe even the early '30s-- it must have been the late '20s-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was, really, the representative institution of the community. Right next to it, was a-- another, smaller known as the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the the home and center and prayer house of what is normally called [NON-ENGLISH], the less observant, less learned, less knowledgeable, but still quite Orthodox elements of the community. The central synagogue was supposedly the home of the more advanced, more enlightened, less Orthodox element in the community. And then there was a major Hasidic synagogue known as the [NON-ENGLISH], which was a kind of a catchall [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] institution, where [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] frequently conflicting Hasidic groups congregated. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Frequently, in, in the [NON-ENGLISH] and around the [NON-ENGLISH], there were quite virulent debates and even, I would say, physical altercations because of the conflicts between the various courts of various rabbis. And then there were a very large number of smaller synagogues, usually attached to the home of one of the Hasidic rabbis in town, who may have been a descendant of very well-known wonder rabbis, but of lesser statue than their predecessors, but who have had their own adherents who congregated in those synagogues. And then there were any number of smaller synagogues, you-- maintained by some private people with means who lived, perhaps, a little too far out so that they could not come during the Sabbath or, or holidays easily by foot and return home to any one of the other synagogues; or who, for a variety of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] personal needs and conveniences, felt that they had to have a prayer house literally in their house or very nearly to it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The educational system-- that really should be called the Jewish system was a, a kind of a four-tiered system where you had the cheders, which youngsters began to attend beginning from age three. I remember when my time came, and my hair was cut, leaving only the payos, at age three at-- with the appropriate celebration, being taken to the cheder that was attached to where Moshe Klein's home, which, in effect, was what might be called pre-kindergarten or, or kindergarten, and where at age three, you were-- began to be taught the Hebrew alphabet and, and reading. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then you had various levels as you advanced in age and knowledge, until you were right for what they called the Talmud Torah, which might be called sort of the middle school. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was-- the, the earlier grades were largely private rabbis or teachers who maintained their own institutions from the fees that the students paid. The Talmud Torah was already a community institution, which, I suppose, was if not assisted financially, certainly supervised by the community and, to some extent, managed by the community, where you had what I-- could be considered regular grades where you advance from one class to the next, dependent on your [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] academic achievement. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I, I must enter here an the incident that occurred to me here maybe about five or six years ago. I had gone to Saint Terese to the [NON-ENGLISH], who, by the way, was a classmate of mine at one point. And when I arrived there, up in the front of his synagogue, there was a man [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] arrayed in the usual Hasidic attire for the holidays, with a stramel and a [NON-ENGLISH] and a long beard fairly well-whitened already. And someone came over to me. And he said that that man up there wants to see me. So I went up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he, he turned to me-- after all that we have gone through, the Holocaust, almost 50 years of living in, in the United States or in North America. And his first words out of his mouth were to me, tell me, when we went to Moshe Klein's Talmud Torah, did we study this tractate of the Talmud or that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I am mentioning this because I think it really, quintessentially demonstrates a Jewish attachment to learning that is so deep-seated that after all that we have gone through, and after a separation and discontinuity of 45, 50 years, the first word out of a man who meets a former fellow student is, what tractate did we study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything else is forgotten. Only the learning matters. Even the suffering is forgotten. And so it is understandable that a culture in, in Hungary was so dominantly [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] created, maintained, and managed by Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To return to the-- to the educational system in Kisvarda, then they were, beyond the Talmud Torah, an-- another grade, so to say, which was considered the Yeshiva K'Tana or the junior high, or, perhaps, junior college. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These were, again, privately run [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] institutions, if you can call them that, because usually what they were-- as were the kindergarten levels also-- they would have room in the home of a particular teacher, who, as I say, derived his living from the teaching and from the fees that his students paid him. And then beyond that, in, in Kisvarda, unlike in some other places where famous rabbis maintain what-- permanently what were known as yeshiva, which you might call graduate schools, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had a-- only occasionally, when our local chief rabbi, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think, was ill, sort of the yeshiva petered out. But the, the interesting thing about this system is-- as well as the life of the community and its organization and structure-- is that it was all contiguous. It was all one. All of the stores on Main Street, with rarest exception, were Jewishly owned. Then, if you turn to the right on Start Street, it was for at least, I would say, a mile, all Jewish residences and institutions. You could go on one side of Start Street and literally go in the backyard of these institutions through that mile or a mile and a half and feel nothing but Jewish institutions and Jewish homes. You started out at the corner with the Jewish delicatessen store and the Jewish grocery store. And then came the Beta Midrash. And then came the central synagogue. And then came the lower classes of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of, of the cheder. And then came the rabbi's residence with his synagogue and-- which also served as the yeshiva. And then you came to the, the Jewish bakery. And then you came to the mikra. And then you came from the mikra. You went to the [? close. ?] And then you went to the-- or in, in the same backyard, you had the, the scribes and his family. And after that, you had the Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] counsel's office. And you had the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the office of the beadles, who, by the way, were also collecting the taxes. It's very interesting-- the system was quite interesting. I, I don't know whether it's really familiar, you know, in North America at all or to North Americans because it's never existed in North America. You were assessed tax. And you could, in effect, elect to pay the tax, either in installment or in one fell swoop. But by and large, most people would pay their tax by going to the beadle's home, who had a little window like a teller's window. And you would buy a ticket to have your chicken slaughtered. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought I have to stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that ticket not only permitted you to have your chickens slaughtered, but at the same time, constituted a payment towards your taxes. And so, in the backyard almost next to the-- to the [? close, ?] there was the slaughterhouse for the chicken. And next to it was the, the concierge, the one non-Jew around, who was responsible for cleaning the slaughterhouse and the synagogues and putting the lights on and off on Sabbath and holidays. He and his family lived there. And they were virtually the only non-Jews for a mile and a half around. And then you had the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, some of the ritual slaughterers were living there. And next to it, next to the beadles, there was a home of the deputy rabbi, the [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Below his building were the butcher shops. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, you know, the butchering of, of animals was under the supervision of the community. A community provided [NON-ENGLISH], who was, of course, paid for by the community. But again, it was paid out of the income paid by the butchers for the slaughtering of the animals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Philip, we're going to take a break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll continue in a moment.
